title: Neutropenic Fever (Febrile Neutropenia)
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Oncology Fellows, Emergency Medicine, Infectious Disease
objectives: Define febrile neutropenia and calculate ANC
Risk stratify patients using MASCC score
Recognize indications for vancomycin and antifungals
Determine when to de-escalate or discontinue antibiotics

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for Antimicrobial Agents in Neutropenic Patients
guidelineReferences.citation: Freifeld AG, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(4):e56-e93
keyPoints: Vancomycin NOT routine - add for specific indications
Empiric antifungals if fever persists 4-7 days

vignette.text:
A 58-year-old woman with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) presents to the ED 10 days after starting induction chemotherapy with fever and chills. She reports fatigue and mild sore throat but denies cough, diarrhea, or dysuria. She has a central venous catheter (port-a-cath) in place.

Past Medical History: AML diagnosed 2 weeks ago, Type 2 diabetes, Hypertension

Medications: Currently on levofloxacin prophylaxis, fluconazole prophylaxis, acyclovir prophylaxis

Allergies: None

Chemotherapy: Cytarabine + daunorubicin (7+3 induction) completed 3 days ago

physicalExam.skin: Port site non-erythematous, no rashes, no perianal tenderness

micro.Blood cultures: Pending (2 sets drawn - peripheral and port)

questions.stem: This patient has fever 39.2°C and ANC 50/μL.
questions.question: How is febrile neutropenia defined?

options.text: ANC <5000 with any temperature
Neutropenia without fever
Any fever in a cancer patient

FEVER: Single oral temp ≥38.3°C (101°F) OR temp ≥38.0°C (100.4°F) sustained for ≥1 hour; NEUTROPENIA: ANC <500/μL OR ANC <1000/μL expected to decline to <500; PROFOUND neutropenia: ANC <100/μL; Calculate ANC = WBC × (% neutrophils + % bands) / 100

questions.explanation: Febrile neutropenia definition (IDSA): FEVER: Single oral temp ≥38.3°C (101°F) OR sustained temp ≥38.0°C for ≥1 hour. NEUTROPENIA: ANC <500 cells/μL OR ANC 500-1000 expected to decline to <500 within 48 hours. PROFOUND neutropenia: ANC <100/μL (highest infection risk). ANC calculation: WBC × (% segs + % bands) / 100. This patient: ANC 50 = profound neutropenia + fever 39.2°C = febrile neutropenia - a MEDICAL EMERGENCY.

whyChain.answer: Without neutrophils, patients cannot mount inflammatory response to infection. Mortality from untreated bacteremia can be 40%+. Signs of infection may be absent (no pus, minimal inflammation). Fever may be the ONLY sign of serious infection. Without empiric antibiotics, sepsis and death can occur rapidly.

questions.stem: You need to determine if this patient is high-risk or low-risk.
questions.question: How do you risk stratify patients with febrile neutropenia?

options.text: Only age determines risk
All febrile neutropenia patients are low-risk

HIGH-RISK: Profound neutropenia (ANC <100) expected >7 days, significant comorbidities (hypotension, pneumonia, new abdominal pain, neurologic changes), MASCC score <21, inpatient status, hematologic malignancy/HSCT; LOW-RISK: Brief neutropenia (<7 days), MASCC ≥21, stable outpatient, solid tumor
Risk stratification is not important

questions.explanation: Risk stratification determines treatment venue and intensity. HIGH-RISK features: Profound neutropenia (ANC ≤100) expected >7 days, Clinically unstable (hypotension, altered mental status), Pneumonia, Significant comorbidities, Hepatic/renal insufficiency, Uncontrolled cancer, MASCC score <21, Inpatient. LOW-RISK: Expected brief neutropenia (<7 days), Solid tumor (not hematologic), MASCC ≥21, No comorbidities, Outpatient. This AML patient post-induction: HIGH-RISK (profound neutropenia, expected prolonged, hematologic malignancy).

whyChain.answer: Multinational Association for Supportive Care in Cancer Risk Index: Burden of illness (5 points mild, 3 moderate), No hypotension (5), No COPD (4), Solid tumor/no prior fungal (4), Outpatient (3), No dehydration (3), Age <60 (2). Score ≥21 = low risk (<5% serious complications). This patient would score <21 = high-risk.

questions.stem: You are initiating the evaluation of this patient.
questions.question: What is the initial workup for febrile neutropenia?

options.text: Blood cultures x2 sets (peripheral AND central line if present) BEFORE antibiotics; CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, lactate; Urinalysis; Chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms; Site-specific cultures based on symptoms; Do NOT delay antibiotics for workup completion
CT scan before any treatment
Only blood cultures are needed
Wait for culture results before antibiotics

questions.explanation: Initial workup: (1) BLOOD CULTURES x2 sets minimum - one from peripheral vein, one from central line (if present) - draw BEFORE antibiotics but do NOT delay treatment; (2) CBC with differential (confirm ANC); (3) Comprehensive metabolic panel (renal/hepatic function); (4) Lactate (sepsis marker); (5) Urinalysis; (6) Chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms; (7) Site-specific cultures (stool if diarrhea, wound cultures, etc.). Key: Draw cultures then START ANTIBIOTICS WITHIN 1 HOUR - do not wait for results.

whyChain.why: Why both peripheral and line cultures?
whyChain.answer: Central line-associated bloodstream infection (CLABSI) is common in neutropenic patients. Differential time to positivity (line positive >2 hours before peripheral) suggests CLABSI. Helps guide whether line removal is needed. This patient has a port - must culture both sites.

sources.text: StatPearls: Neutropenic Fever. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Blood cultures are drawn. You need to start antibiotics immediately.
questions.question: What is the recommended empiric antibiotic regimen for high-risk febrile neutropenia?

options.text: ANTIPSEUDOMONAL BETA-LACTAM MONOTHERAPY: Cefepime 2g IV q8h, OR Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h, OR Meropenem 1g IV q8h, OR Imipenem-cilastatin 500mg IV q6h; Start within 1 HOUR of presentation; Vancomycin NOT routinely added
Wait for culture results to choose antibiotics
Vancomycin alone is first-line
Oral antibiotics for all patients

questions.explanation: Empiric antibiotics for HIGH-RISK febrile neutropenia: ANTIPSEUDOMONAL BETA-LACTAM MONOTHERAPY preferred: Cefepime 2g IV q8h (most commonly used), Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h, Meropenem 1g IV q8h (if ESBL concern), Imipenem-cilastatin 500mg IV q6h. Must cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa - historically most lethal pathogen in neutropenic fever. START WITHIN 1 HOUR of triage. Vancomycin NOT recommended routinely - add only for specific indications.

whyChain.answer: Studies show no mortality benefit from empiric vancomycin. Increases nephrotoxicity, VRE selection, and cost. Add vancomycin only for: suspected CLABSI, skin/soft tissue infection, hemodynamic instability, known MRSA colonization, severe mucositis with concern for viridans streptococci.

whyChain.why: What about this patient on levofloxacin prophylaxis?
whyChain.answer: Patients on fluoroquinolone prophylaxis should NOT receive fluoroquinolone-based empiric therapy (breakthrough organisms likely resistant). She should receive IV antipseudomonal beta-lactam. If using outpatient regimen, cannot use fluoroquinolone + amoxicillin-clavulanate.

questions.stem: The patient becomes hypotensive despite fluid resuscitation.
questions.question: When should vancomycin or other agents be added to empiric therapy?

options.text: Add vancomycin to all patients
Vancomycin only after culture confirmation
Never add vancomycin

ADD VANCOMYCIN for: Hemodynamic instability/sepsis, Suspected catheter-related infection, Skin/soft tissue infection, Pneumonia, Known MRSA colonization, Severe mucositis (viridans strep); ADD AMINOGLYCOSIDE or second gram-negative agent for septic shock; Consider double gram-negative coverage if resistant organisms suspected

questions.explanation: Indications to EXPAND empiric coverage: ADD VANCOMYCIN: Hemodynamic instability/septic shock, Suspected catheter-related bloodstream infection, Skin/soft tissue infection, Pneumonia (especially in setting of MRSA risk), Known MRSA colonization, Severe mucositis (viridans streptococci can cause septic shock). ADD AMINOGLYCOSIDE or second gram-negative agent: Septic shock, Suspected resistant gram-negatives. This patient with hypotension needs vancomycin added and possibly aminoglycoside for double gram-negative coverage.

questions.stem: The patient remains febrile after 5 days of broad-spectrum antibiotics with negative cultures.
questions.question: When should empiric antifungal therapy be added?

options.text: Never add antifungals empirically
Only fluconazole is effective
Antifungals should be started immediately

Add empiric antifungal after 4-7 days of persistent fever on broad-spectrum antibiotics in high-risk patients (prolonged neutropenia); Options: Caspofungin, Micafungin, Liposomal amphotericin B, Voriconazole; Consider earlier if high fungal risk (prior fungal infection, prolonged steroids, HSCT)

questions.explanation: Empiric antifungal therapy: Add after 4-7 days of persistent fever on appropriate antibacterial therapy in HIGH-RISK patients with expected prolonged neutropenia (>7 days). Covers invasive aspergillosis and candidiasis. Options: ECHINOCANDINS (caspofungin, micafungin) - good for Candida and some Aspergillus, Liposomal amphotericin B - broad spectrum, Voriconazole - covers Aspergillus. Note: This patient is on fluconazole prophylaxis - if adding empiric antifungal, choose different class (echinocandin or amphotericin) to cover resistant/breakthrough organisms.

whyChain.answer: Consider: Serum galactomannan (Aspergillus), Beta-D-glucan (pan-fungal), CT chest (pulmonary aspergillosis shows halo sign, air-crescent), CT sinuses if symptoms. Bronchoalveolar lavage if pulmonary infiltrates. Don't wait for definitive diagnosis if high clinical suspicion.

questions.stem: The patient defervesces after 3 days, cultures remain negative, and ANC is rising.
questions.question: When can antibiotics be discontinued in febrile neutropenia?

options.text: Always continue for 14 days
Continue until discharge regardless of counts
Stop immediately when afebrile

Traditional: Continue until ANC ≥500 AND afebrile ≥48 hours AND clinically stable; Newer evidence supports earlier discontinuation if: Afebrile ≥48-72 hours, Clinically stable, No documented infection, ANC recovering (even if <500); De-escalate to prophylaxis if neutropenia persists but stable

questions.explanation: Antibiotic duration: TRADITIONAL approach: Continue until ANC ≥500/μL AND afebrile for 48 hours AND clinically stable. NEWER evidence: May discontinue after 72 hours if afebrile, stable, no documented infection - even if ANC still <500 (with close monitoring). If documented infection: Complete appropriate course based on organism and site. If persistent neutropenia but stable and afebrile: Consider de-escalation to prophylaxis with close monitoring. This patient: If afebrile 48h, cultures negative, ANC rising - can consider stopping empiric antibiotics.

sources.text: StatPearls: Neutropenic Fever. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient: low-risk febrile neutropenia with solid tumor and expected brief neutropenia.
questions.question: Who can be managed as an outpatient and with what regimen?

options.text: All febrile neutropenia patients need admission
Outpatients don't need antibiotics
IV antibiotics only option

LOW-RISK criteria: MASCC ≥21, Expected neutropenia <7 days, No comorbidities, Solid tumor, Outpatient at onset, Clinically stable; Empiric: Fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin OR levofloxacin) + amoxicillin-clavulanate (or clindamycin if PCN allergic); First dose in clinic, observe 4 hours, close follow-up

questions.explanation: Outpatient management for LOW-RISK patients: Criteria: MASCC ≥21, Expected short neutropenia (<7 days), Solid tumor, No significant comorbidities, Outpatient at fever onset, Good social support, Access to medical care. Regimen: Ciprofloxacin 500-750mg PO q12h + Amoxicillin-clavulanate 500mg PO q8h (or clindamycin 300mg q6h if PCN allergic). Protocol: First dose in clinic/ED, observe 4 hours minimum, daily follow-up. Re-evaluate for admission if not improved in 2-3 days. NOT for patients on FQ prophylaxis.

questions.stem: Returning to the original AML patient.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach for this patient?

options.text: Observation without antibiotics
Wait for culture results
Oral antibiotics and discharge

Immediate (within 1 hour): Blood cultures x2 sets (peripheral + port), start Cefepime 2g IV q8h (or pip-tazo/carbapenem); Add vancomycin given hypotension; Workup: CBC, CMP, lactate, UA, CXR; Supportive care: Fluids, vasopressors if needed; If persistent fever day 4-7: Add empiric antifungal; Continue until ANC ≥500 + afebrile 48h + stable

questions.explanation: Complete management for this HIGH-RISK patient: (1) IMMEDIATE (<1 hour): Draw blood cultures (peripheral + port), Start cefepime 2g IV q8h (avoid fluoroquinolone given prophylaxis), Add vancomycin given hypotension and mucositis, IV fluids for hypotension; (2) WORKUP: CBC, CMP, lactate, UA, CXR (all done); (3) MONITORING: ICU if hemodynamically unstable, daily assessment; (4) IF PERSISTENT FEVER day 4-7: Add antifungal (echinocandin preferred given fluconazole prophylaxis), Consider CT chest for pulmonary infiltrates; (5) DURATION: Continue antibiotics until ANC ≥500, afebrile 48h, clinically stable, or treat documented infection appropriately.

sources.text: StatPearls: Neutropenic Fever. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected neutropenic fever?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in neutropenic fever. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of neutropenic fever. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing neutropenic fever, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old presents with petechiae, confusion, renal insufficiency, fever, and platelets of 18,000 with schistocytes on smear. What is the most critical immediate intervention?

options.text: Emergent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) — do not wait for ADAMTS13 results
options.explanation: This pentad (thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, fever) is classic TTP. Per ASH guidelines, plasma exchange must be initiated IMMEDIATELY — mortality without treatment exceeds 90%. Do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 activity levels; start treatment on clinical suspicion.

options.text: Platelet transfusion to prevent intracranial hemorrhage
options.explanation: Platelet transfusion in TTP is CONTRAINDICATED (relative) as it can fuel thrombotic microangiopathy and worsen organ damage. Platelets are consumed in microvascular thrombi, not bleeding.

options.text: Start heparin for presumed DIC
options.explanation: TTP is distinguished from DIC by NORMAL coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen). DIC shows prolonged PT/PTT and low fibrinogen. Treatment differs fundamentally — TTP requires plasmapheresis, not anticoagulation.

options.text: Obtain ADAMTS13 activity level and treat based on results
options.explanation: ADAMTS13 testing takes 2-3 days. With suspected TTP, mortality approaches 90% without treatment. Plasma exchange must begin on clinical suspicion while awaiting confirmatory testing.

sections.title: Clinical Approach
sections.content: Systematic approach to Neutropenic Fever including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content: Evidence-based clinical pearls for Neutropenic Fever.
